Title: Scene-Anchored Analysis of Affective “Stickiness” in Nordic Political-Thriller Television with Large Language Models
Topics: computer and data sciences cultural studies large language models media studies social sciences text analysis
Abstract: This paper examines whether large language models (LLMs) reproduce affective stickiness (Ahmed, 2014), the tendency of emotions such as fear or suspicion to adhere to racialized and gendered bodies, when generating narratives about Muslim women. Conducting decontextualized bias tests, it situates analysis within Nordic television thrillers Caliphate (Sweden, 2020) and Bullets (Finland, 2018). Two complementary experiments combine narrative prompting, attribution analysis, and embedding analysis using SBERT. In the first, identical scenes featuring different character identities are extended by ChatGPT, LLaMA, and Mistral to reveal how identity shapes emotional tone. In the second, models describe what stands out about characters, allowing comparison between explicit attributions and generative behavior. Preliminary findings show that LLMs consistently attach fear and suspicion to Muslim-identified characters through carriers such as the hijab, while similar cues for Nordic women remain neutral. The study connects computational auditing and humanities interpretation to expose affective bias in AI storytelling.

Title: AI-powered Storytelling: bridging museum collections and family archives to implement anti-racist teaching practices
Topics: educational studies GLAM information retrieval knowledge representation large language models media studies other
Abstract: AI-powered Storytelling is a collaborative digital storytelling project idea involving UK universities, museums, oral history initiatives, and schools. It aims to promote anti-racist and inclusive teaching by co-developing AI tools and ethical protocols that enable the co-creation and sharing of complex or contested narratives inspired by museum artefacts and oral histories. The project builds on pilots with the London Transport Museum and The Making of Black Britain, addressing critical needs for accessible tools to help teachers decolonise the curriculum and support students—especially those from diverse linguistic backgrounds—with generative AI and digital storytelling. Key activities include curating culturally diverse datasets, generating AI video summaries, and piloting digital storytelling methods. The initiative also explores public and educational uses of AI-powered storytelling, such as museum engagement and unpacking complex narratives, ensuring that both students and educators responsibly co-create content informed by cultural heritage and community histories.

Authors: David Zbíral, Zoltan Brys, Robert L. J. Shaw and Gideon Kotzé
Title: Using LLMs to uncover hidden patterns in the contestation of religious authorities across a corpus of medieval inquisition records, 1243–1522
Topics: history information retrieval large language models religious studies statistics and data science
Abstract: This study applies large language models (LLMs) to extract and analyze sparse evidence of direct contestation of religious authorities in 4,357 documents from 20 inquisition registers (1243–1522). While traditional methods struggle to detect such dispersed expressions, LLM-assisted classification enables large-scale quantitative assessment of instances where individuals explicitly challenged ecclesiastical power. Regression and random forest analyses reveal that reformistic milieus (Beguins, Lollards) were significantly more likely to express such contestation than separatistic ones (Cathars, Guglielmites, Apostles). Unexpectedly, urban registers show lower incidence, whereas later-period records show an increase, aligning with the growth of the late medieval public sphere. Gender showed no significant effect. The study demonstrates how LLM-based information extraction—combined with careful validation—can uncover broad cultural patterns in historical resistance otherwise inaccessible through close reading.

Title: Rewriting the Past for the Present: A Computational Study of Sitcom Presentism using Fine-Tuned Language Models
Topics: computer and data sciences large language models literary studies media studies statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: We model presentism (reinterpreting past artefacts through contemporary horizons [1, 2]) as a computational pipeline that updates 1990s Seinfeld plots to read plausibly in 2025 while preserving comedic beats and ensemble dynamics. Stage (1) rewrites Wikipedia-style outlines into 4–8-beat 2025 plot sketches with a style card and the exact stop marker ### Plot End. Stage (2) uses a high-precision NER prompt to extract entities in a fixed 18-key schema, deduplicated with fallbacks. Stage (3) builds entity-aware IO pairs and fine-tunes Qwen2.5-14B Instruct with PEFT (AdaLoRA) in 4-bit NF4. Averaging five independent 4-fold evaluations (20 runs total), final loss is 1.336 (std 0.040); best loss 1.329 (std 0.036). Outputs are compact (mean length ≈ 181 tokens), semantically coherent with inputs (0.553 cosine), and show low lexical overlap (Jaccard 0.132). Diversity remains high (distinct-2 = 0.721; distinct-3 = 0.927). Materials, prompts, and evaluation scripts will be released via Anonymous Repository.

Authors: Annemieke Romein, Melissa Terras, Andy Stauder, Florian Stauder and Michaela Prien
Title: "As Open as Possible, as Closed as Necessary": Balancing Openness, Sustainability, and Data Protection in Cooperative AI Infrastructure
Topics: computer and data sciences GLAM history information sciences open science and public humanities
Abstract: READ-COOP, a European Cooperative Society established in 2019, maintains Transkribus, a machine learning platform for automated recognition of text in historical documents. The cooperative navigates a critical tension between openness and the practical necessities of data protection, intellectual property preservation, and financial sustainability. Whilst complete openness risks exploitation by larger technology providers and threatens the cooperative's viability, READ-COOP maintains maximum accessibility through free monthly credits, a comprehensive Scholarship Programme supporting students from 73 countries, user data ownership, and 300 licensed AI models for community use. The cooperative structure embodies democratic governance, enabling meaningful participation by members in platform development. This approach demonstrates that ethical AI governance requires both technical openness and structural mechanisms ensuring community participation and accountability. READ-COOP's experience suggests that responsible AI development necessitates a pragmatic balance rather than absolutist positions, offering an alternative framework that protects both technology and community while ensuring long-term viability and broad accessibility.

Title: Multimodal Digital Storytelling and Interaction: A Historical Perspective from the ZKM
Topics: cultural studies data visualization GLAM media studies
Abstract: The proposed presentation examines digital storytelling and interaction at the ZKM (Center for Art and Media Karlsruhe), which evolved in close dialogue with implementing the institutional goals. Today, the ZKM operates as a hybrid cognitive environment, where various narratives are designed and distributed across digital and physical domains and reflect onsite, online, and hybrid modes of interaction. Research explores how ZKM’s narratives have evolved into immersive, algorithmic, and networked forms of audience interaction. Drawing on qualitative and data-driven perspectives, the historical overview identifies key milestones and tendencies, from early media art web-experiments to recent hybrid projects based on AI, highlighting shifts in how stories are produced, mediated, and experienced. The study explores the ZKM’s live and archived web, including the main website, satellite web-based projects and their snapshots on the Internet Archive, and complements these materials with other institutional outputs such as catalogues, releases and other records.

Title: Unsmurfed: How an LLM Interprets the Smurfs’ Distributional Language
Topics: large language models linguistics literary studies media studies text analysis
Abstract: Many foundational LLMs such as BERT are trained on masked-language modeling, which consists in guessing masked words in a sentence, building on Firth’s distributional semantics (“You shall know a word by the company it keeps”). We use the French model CamemBERT to replace every occurrence of the word schtroumpf- ("smurf-") with its contextually most probable word in a corpus of 300 pages (five albums) of the Belgian Smurfs comics. Our multimodal pipeline consists of comic-centered OCR, image captioning and automated token prediction. By generating ten versions of all speech bubbles in about 3000 panels (top-1 to 10 most likely predictions), our experiment exposes how an LLM performs the very task that enables humans to naturally understand the Smurfs’ playful language: inferring meaning from context. The resulting “un-smurfed” narratives highlight the interpretive constraints of LLMs and point to how reader agency can be weakened when generative AI intervenes in storytelling.

Title: Narrative construction of “AI” through anthropomorphising language in German public discourse
Topics: linguistics media studies text analysis
Abstract: This paper examines narratives about “AI” as a human-like actor in the German public discourse across media and over time. We define narratives as recurring lexico-grammatical patterns that anthropomorphise AI and analyse 41,358 deduplicated sentences containing the word AI from quality newspapers, IT blogs, and web texts (2019–2024). Combining spaCy-based NLP with manual annotation, we investigate: (1) anthropomorphic lexemes in the immediate context of AI (agent, cognizer, communicator, biological metaphor), (2) the syntactic role of AI (subject vs. object), and (3) pronominal reference to AI. Results show that agency is the dominant category, with newspapers becoming the primary locus of anthropomorphising language after the release of ChatGPT. Pronominal reference to AI remains rare but increases slightly over time, especially in the press. We argue that these patterns constitute “distantly read” AI narratives that shape expectations about AI’s autonomy, cognitive, and communicative capabilities.

Authors: C. Annemieke Romein, Jos Mooijweer and Andreas Weber
Title: Unlocking Provincial Voices: How ML/AI Enables New Narratives of Citizenship in the Early Modern Dutch Republic. The Case of Overijssel’s Resolutions (1578-1795).
Topics: history information retrieval other spatial analysis text analysis
Abstract: This research unlocks the political voices of Overijssel through ML/AI applied to the Resolutions of the States of Overijssel (1578-1795), comprising over 50,000 handwritten pages documenting provincial governance. The corpus contains untold stories of petitioners seeking justice, communities negotiating with authorities, and regions asserting their political voice. The technical pipeline combines automated handwritten text recognition through Transkribus, sophisticated layout analysis, and dual-pathway Named Entity Recognition. Citizen scientists validate entities manually through Transkribus, enhanced by custom Flair models trained on historical Dutch administrative texts from the REPUBLIC project. Geographical relationships are systematically linked to Wikidata identifiers, enabling spatial analysis of political participation across Overijssel's regions. This human-AI collaboration transforms inaccessible manuscripts into structured, queryable data in PageXML format. Initial findings show which regions exercised political voice through petitioning and how participation evolved during two centuries of crisis and transformation. The methodology is likely transferable to other provincial archives.

Authors: Jaap Geraerts, Henry Keazor, Demival Vasques Filho, Rebecca Welkens and Thorsten Wübbena
Title: Forgeries and Networks (ForNet). The Mittheilungen des Museen-Verbandes and forgery networks in the 20th century
Topics: art history data visualization network analysis
Abstract: The DFG-funded ForNet project analyzes the Mittheilungen des Museen-Verbandes (1899-1939), a publication by the International Association of Museum Officials (founded 1898), to reconstruct historical networks of art forgery and institutional secrecy. This poster presentation focuses on the methodological framework we develop, starting from turning archival data into semi-structured data, enabling performing network analysis that, in turn, ends up leading to more archival research. By creating XML transcriptions from digitized source materials and importing them into a graph database, the project enables the visualization of personnel networks, organizational structures, and forgery-related actor constellations. The study applies advanced network analysis techniques to uncover decision-making processes, central actors, and the interplay between institutional growth and forgery detection.

Title: Airships XR: Applied Data Visualization to Diagnose the Failure of Ambition
Topics: data visualization educational studies GLAM history knowledge representation open science and public humanities
Abstract: The "Airships XR: Applied Data Visualization to Diagnose the Failure of Ambition" project offers an interactive demonstration focused on the 1930 crash of the R101 airship, contrasting it with the sustainable Airlander 10. The R101 is framed as a "Hopeful Monstrosity" whose failure was caused by external pressures and pride collectively superseding safety engineering. The demonstration, developed at the RCA and leveraging HTC Viverse and bespoke Mixed-reality airship experiences, allows delegates to visually analyse structural failures. Seven interactive stations explore forensic data, covering issues like the R101's overweight diesel engines, ignored gas bag damage, and political ambition driving an early, fatal departure. The project, a recipient of the Ingenious Award, aims to use this narrative to broaden perceptions of engineering and advocate for STEAM.

Authors: Georgia Sivri, Afroditi Kamara and Despoina Lampada
Title: From Memory to Multimodal Storytelling: Digitally Narrating Asia Minor Urban Culture at ESTIA Neas Smyrnis
Topics: cultural studies educational studies GLAM history information retrieval infrastructure and tools knowledge representation
Abstract: This presentation examines ESTIA Neas Smyrnis’ digital heritage initiative Asia Minor Culture and Cosmopolitanism, developed in the aftermath of the centenary of the Asia Minor Catastrophe. Through the DigiSmALL platform, 117 artifacts from five museum sections were digitized, documented, and contextualized according to Europeana and SearchCulture standards, transforming previously inaccessible collections into open digital resources. An immersive exhibition created with DigiSmALL and ©Artsteps reframed Asia Minor cosmopolitanism through interactive, non-linear storytelling. Complementary educational tools—including a card-based game, an experiential museum program, and a thematic guided tour—fostered intergenerational dialogue and historical empathy. Emphasizing ethical representation and plural refugee narratives, the project highlighted women’s creativity and resilience and inspired future digital exhibitions on gender, textiles, and diaspora memory. Overall, the project demonstrates how multimodal digital storytelling can preserve cultural heritage, activate community memory, and empower small GLAM institutions through accessible, narrative-driven digital humanities practice.

Title: Rethinking metadata in a large crowdsourced Second World War archive
Topics: GLAM history infrastructure and tools large language models text analysis visual analysis
Abstract: This paper examines how metadata shapes digital storytelling in large crowdsourced archives, drawing on three strands of work with the University of Oxford's Their Finest Hour project. It reflects on the practical challenges of collecting and presenting more than 25,000 family stories and digitised artefacts from the Second World War, where uneven detail, mixed voices, and varied styles influence how personal memories become a shared historical record. It then considers an AI-assisted keyword project that tested whether automated suggestions could enrich, rather than replace, human tagging, highlighting how inconsistent descriptions limit traditional NLP methods and how all keywords steer interpretation. Finally, it evaluates AI-based visual-analysis tools used on poorly catalogued photographs to produce richer, more consistent descriptions and identify identities, settings, and forms of commemoration. The paper concludes with principles for designing metadata that acknowledges uncertainty, avoids over-interpretation, and supports user-generated narratives.

Authors: Bas Vercruysse, Vincent Ducatteeuw, Julie Birkholz, Christophe Verbruggen, Johan Poukens, Heike Bekaert and Jahid Chetti
Title: Narrating Belgian Business History
Topics: computer and data sciences data visualization history infrastructure and tools knowledge representation large language models open science and public humanities social sciences
Abstract: The [anonymized] project transforms over two centuries of Belgian corporate records into structured data, integrating them into the established [anonymized] firm-level research database. Published since 1873 in the Annexes of the Belgian Official Gazette (Le Moniteur Belge), these legal notices contain rich information on incorporations, capital structures, financial statements, securities holdings, mergers, and bankruptcies. Despite their historical value, their volume and heterogeneity have prevented systematic analysis. This project converts millions of archival fragments into a research infrastructure, linking them into coherent firm trajectories and networks spanning decades. We illustrate our approach through case studies including Groupe Bruxelles Lambert (GBL). The presentation outlines our digital workflow: high-resolution scanning, automatic text recognition, named entity recognition, data reconciliation with existing datasets, and release as linked data on an interactive platform. The project demonstrates how large-scale archival digitization transforms complex corporate records into accessible narratives, enabling new forms of historical analysis and digital storytelling.

Title: Detecting multilingualism in early modern drama
Topics: history literary studies text analysis
Abstract: This paper aims to quantify multilingualism in early modern drama by automatically detecting the languages spoken by 33,250 speakers from 2501 early modern plays written in 6 languages: French, English, German, Spanish, Dutch and Italian from 1500-1800. The frequency of detected non-native speech offers an indication of the cultural and linguistic diversity represented on the stage in each literary tradition, which possibly reflects the specific language diversity that is typical to the cultural-historical context of each tradition. Besides these language-specific differences, descriptive statistics indicate that Latin speakers are relatively common in all traditions. Analysing the speeches detected as Latin, this paper argues that Latin lines are often spoken by stock characters such as the ‘pedant’, the quack, or the imposter scholar. The prominence of these characters and their Latinate gibberish are read as expressions of cross-cultural scepticism regarding members of the learned classes and scholarly knowledge itself.

Title: Visualising the Impacts of Environmental and Infrastructural Deprivation on Poverty in India
Topics: data visualization visual analysis
Abstract: This project explores how environmental and social datasets can be transformed into interactive narratives that reveal spatial inequality. The Multidimensional Poverty Index (MPI) data (2019–2021) is combined with environmental and infrastructure variables, such as air pollution (PM2.5 exposure), electricity facility, drinking water facility, and electricity facility, using India as a case study. Through data and visualisations, the project assesses how pollution and infrastructure facilities impacts the poverty.

Title: AI-Assisted NER/NED in ARACNE+EVT for Digital Critical Editions and Cultural Heritage
Topics: cultural studies GLAM information retrieval literary studies open science and public humanities text analysis
Abstract: This paper presents (MYPROJECTANONIM.) as both an infrastructure and a set of experiments at the intersection of digital critical editing and AI. First, it introduces ARACNE, an XML-TEI/Linked Open Data framework based on eXist-db and XQuery, integrated with Edition Visualization Technology (EVT) to publish late medieval and Renaissance sources as open-access, TEI-driven critical editions with facsimiles, apparatus and stable citation. Second, it reports on experiments in ChatGPT-assisted automated markup, Named-Entity Recognition (NER) and Named-Entity Disambiguation (NED). These tests were carried out on corpora in which many entities had already been encoded in XML-TEI with @ref attributes pointing to Wikidata, giving the model a dense semantic context. This setting allowed targeted exploitation of the semantic web, yielding high-quality suggestions and substantial time savings. The paper argues that such human-supervised AI workflows open new frontiers for online data publication and, consequently, for the preservation of cultural heritage.

Title: From Postbag to Portal: Digital Storytelling with Convict Letters from HMS Calcutta 1803
Topics: data visualization history knowledge representation open science and public humanities
Abstract: This lightning talk presents an emerging digital storytelling platform built around a collection of previously unopened letters written in 1803 by convicts, soldiers, and crew aboard HMS Calcutta en route to Australia. Preserved at The National Archives, UK, these letters are being catalogued and edited for a digital edition that combines close reading with quantitative methods to explore how criminalization and forced migration were narrated in transit and can be re-mediated today through interactive, data-rich interfaces. Drawing on the letters as well as on logbooks, court records, person-based datasets, and existing convict databases, the project develops a prosopographical model in which each letter becomes a narrative node navigable via biographical metadata. The talk showcases a prototype open access platform with facsimiles, transcriptions, and visualizations, and reflects on design decisions, early findings, and the potential of metadata as a narrative engine for multivocal histories of forced migration at sea.

Authors: Alie Lassche, Mika Kipke, Rie Schmidt Eriksen, Katrine Baunvig and Kristoffer Nielbo
Title: Corsaren: Multimodal Storytelling in Nineteenth Century Danish Satire
Topics: art history cultural studies history large language models linguistics media studies text analysis visual analysis
Abstract: This paper presents a first computational study of the Danish satirical weekly Corsaren (1840–1855), a key publication from Europe’s “golden age of caricature”. Satire in Corsaren emerges from the interplay of text and image, requiring readers to navigate shifting layers of critique, irony, and visual metaphor. We approach the journal as an intermedial narrative form, and address in this paper two initial steps towards anchoring image and text in Corsaren to understand how satire is conveyed. We do that through a lexical and semantic analysis of Corsaren's text using text classification tasks, and through an exploration of Corsaren's illustrations using image clusters.

Title: Narrative mapping of historical data: Tracing known locations of the Legendary 18th Century Bokkenrijders in Limburg
Topics: cultural studies data visualization history media studies network analysis spatial analysis
Abstract: This presentation focuses on the story of the Bokkenrijders, a criminal phenomenon that terrorised the Limburg region in the Lands of Overmaas, County of Looz, and the western part of Germany between circa 1734 and 1795. This research involved a collaboration across digital humanities and historiography through a close reading of primary and secondary sources and using data visualisation methods. Based on S.J.P. Sleinada’s publication (1779), which includes a list of accused and convicted Bokkenrijders, a digital map was created using Nodegoat software and a series of hierarchy maps of the accused. The aim of this mapping was to answer two research questions: (1) Where were Bokkenrijders operating in the Limburg region? (2) How did their geographical reach lead to reports that the  Bokkenrijders were flying on goats in the night to conduct their crimes? This mapping represents one of the findings from a larger research project on the Bokkenrijders.

Authors: Loren Verreyen, Melvin Wevers, Thomas Smits and Vincent Kuitenbrouwer
Title: The playlist pipeline: Reconstructing the historical music programming of the Indonesian programme of Radio Netherlands Wereldomroep
Topics: history large language models media studies text analysis visual analysis
Abstract: Radio programming strategies are central to understanding how international broadcasting served as a tool of soft power and public diplomacy. Despite its significance, radio historians have largely overlooked this topic due to the abundance and heterogeneity of source materials such as logbooks and radio guides. This paper presents a computational pipeline to reconstruct the historical playlists of the Indonesian programme of Radio Netherlands Wereldomroep (RNW) between 1950 and 1969. Drawing on a complete set of digitized daily broadcast reports, we employ vision-language models to extract structured schedule data, large language models to parse record identifiers from free-text remarks, and automated linking to the Discogs database for metadata retrieval. Validated against manually annotated 1965 broadcasts, this approach enables systematic analysis of music and genre trends transmitted to Indonesian audiences over two decades, offering new insights into Dutch cultural diplomacy efforts following Indonesian independence.

Authors: Charles van Den Heuvel, Sofia Baroncini and Veruska Zamborlini
Title: Creating Authentic Stories in a Cultural Heritage Knowledge Architecture: Modeling 3D reconstructions, replicas and restorations of the Chest of Distress of Maastricht
Topics: art history cultural studies history knowledge representation religious studies
Abstract: During restorations the values of cultural objects and their uses are renegotiated. It  results in new stories to underpin arguments for keeping or changing the original identity of these objects. A much discussed, even contested term in this context is “authenticity”. We propose modeling (different views on) various concepts of authenticity to investigate the impact of restoration changes on the original identity of cultural artefacts and immaterial expressions related to them. To this end, we explore the potential to integrate a 3D reconstruction of the history and restoration of the 12th century reliquary shrine of Saint Servatius in Maastricht in a semantic web knowledge graph. By modelling different interpretations of the concept authenticity in storylines and by exploring integrations hereof in existing ontological models of creative processes, 3D models, and restoration processes we hope to contribute to a meaningful knowledge architecture.

Authors: Shuai Wang, Costas Papadopoulos and Pedro Hernández Serrano
Title: Applying Data Infrastructure Maturity Profile to Digital Humanities
Topics: computer and data sciences information sciences infrastructure and tools open science and public humanities
Abstract: In this extended abstract, we introduce Data Infrastructure Maturity Profile (DIMP) and provide its first application in the digital humanities domain. More specifically, we provide details on its use for the PURE3D infrastructure.

Authors: Feruza Bakhtiyorova, Dunya Boon and Fangru Li
Title: Workforce Imbalance and Structural Decline: A  Data-Driven Analysis of the Dutch East India  Company (1700-1780)
Topics: cultural studies data visualization history knowledge representation open science and public humanities social sciences statistics and data science visual analysis
Abstract: This study analyzes eighteenth-century workforce patterns within the Dutch East India Company (VOC) using an enriched historical dataset of 670,000 contracts from 1700 to 1780 (Petram et al., 2024). Historians such as Gaastra (2007) have linked the company’s decline to external pressures, including rising warfare, growing financial difficulties, and intensifying global competition. This research investigates how internal labor structures may also have contributed to the VOC’s long-term instability. Descriptive analysis and two predictive models, Random Forest and Logistic Regression, reveal persistent structural problems: high mortality in most ranks, insufficient recruitment in several key occupations, and unequal representation across regions and ranks. These results indicate that the VOC struggled to maintain a stable workforce, which limited its ability to respond to external challenges. The study shows how quantitative and historical methods together can reveal internal vulnerabilities in early modern organizations.

Title: Turning Lists into Narratives: A Data-Driven History of the Press in Modern China (1931-1937)
Topics: computer and data sciences data visualization history information retrieval media studies social sciences spatial analysis statistics and data science visual analysis
Abstract: Drawing on Carl Crow’s Newspaper Directory of China (1931–1937), this paper employs data visualization and statistical modeling to reconstruct the development of the early twentieth-century Chinese press. Covering more than 700 periodicals, the directories reveal rapid expansion (from 367 titles in 1931 to 744 in 1935) coupled with high volatility, as only 262 persisted across editions. Growth concentrated in treaty ports, especially Shanghai, and the market remained dominated by Chinese-language daily newspapers. Emerging intermediate periodicities and diverse foreign-language clusters reflected increasing segmentation and colonial geographies. Material analysis indicates growing standardization of format and layout, while circulation stayed modest (1,000–5,000 copies for most) despite an estimated 9–18 million daily readers. Publisher data show the rising role of non-professional, civil-society, and state actors during the Nanjing decade. Multivariate and text analyses identify distinct press clusters and naming patterns. A companion Shiny application enables interactive exploration of these findings.

Title: Digital Heresy: Let the Machine Misjudge the Law
Topics: art history infrastructure and tools media studies open science and public humanities religious studies visual analysis
Abstract: This project creates a trilogy of experimental animations that reinterpret three religious murals produced in 1961 for the Tribunal da Relação do Porto. Generative AI converts sacred iconography into a contemporary judicial narrative and exposes a legal system dependent on documentation, visibility, and procedural force. A judge, a police officer, and one accused citizen move through accusation, judgment, and sentencing. Their world mutates because the images result from probabilistic computation rather than intention. Errors and distortions interrupt institutional order and open narrative space where technology destabilizes authority. Human authorship asserts itself through selection and contextual framing, while the machine operates without agency. Exhibited in the courthouse that hosts the original murals, the work positions digital storytelling as a tool to confront power, legitimacy, and the fragile promises of automation. Current stage: production completed; theoretical consolidation underway.

Title: Recovering Fragmented Lives: Storytelling the Lives of Shanghai’s Shopkeepers
Topics: data visualization history information retrieval infrastructure and tools large language models network analysis spatial analysis text analysis
Abstract: Urban shopkeepers—grocers, rice merchants, tailors, pawnbrokers, food sellers, stationers, silk retailers, and many others—played a central yet understudied role in the making of modern Shanghai. Although omnipresent in everyday life, they remain largely absent from dominant urban narratives. This project uses a large multilingual newspaper corpus (69,000 articles) from 1890 to 1949 to reconstruct their experiences and collective presence through digital, data-driven storytelling. By combining entity extraction, transformer-based classification, topic modelling, and spatial and temporal reconstruction, it identifies the narrative patterns and socio-spatial configurations that shaped shopkeeping life, including credit, taxation, guild conflict, neighborhood sociability, market volatility, and wartime disruption. Constrained LLM-based narrative synthesis, grounded exclusively in extracted evidence, generates micro-stories that illuminate how these actors navigated shifting political and economic conditions. The project demonstrates how computational methods can recover muted voices and produce historically grounded narratives that expand our understanding of Shanghai’s urban society.

Title: Computational Modeling of Diachronic Variation in Late and Medieval Latin
Topics: history linguistics statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: Can a computer determine when a Latin text was written? To explore this question, we assess the feasibility of automatically dating historical Latin writings based solely on linguistic features, addressing a methodological gap in computational philology where stylometric techniques (commonly employed for authorship attribution) have rarely been applied to chronological classification. Drawing on a large corpus of Late and Medieval Latin, we investigate whether syntactic patterns exhibit systematic diachronic variation. A prototype model distinguishes between two distant periods (4th–5th vs. 11th–12th centuries) using part-of-speech bigram frequencies and a linear SVM classifier, achieving 86.9% accuracy. Analysis of bigram distributions reveals interpretable structural shifts, suggesting that syntactic tendencies can function as reliable and transparent chronological markers even in the absence of lexical information. Future work will refine temporal granularity and integrate additional linguistic dimensions, aiming to improve predictive performance while maintaining philological interpretability.

Authors: Robert L. J. Shaw, Tomáš Hampejs and David Zbíral
Title: Narratives of religious dissidence in medieval inquisition records: computing the representation and sequencing of crimes in Peter Seila's register of sentences (1241–2)
Topics: data visualization history information retrieval infrastructure and tools knowledge representation religious studies statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: This paper presents the first computational narrative analysis of medieval inquisition trial records. We examine Peter Seila's register of sentences (1241–2, Southern France), containing 649 summaries of guilt, rendering the text in 10,000 data statements via Computer-Assisted Semantic Text Modelling (CASTEMO). We reveal how stories of religious dissidence were constructed through inquisitor, notary, and suspects interacting. A narrow crime vocabulary underpins the register's formulaic feel, yet semantic framing patterns illuminate multivocality: recurrence expressions suggest notarial aggregation, while circumstantial details reflect inquisitorial probing and suspects' explanations. Later summaries become shorter and more standardized. Formal conformity testing demonstrates their sequencing alignment with the c. 1244 Narbonne Order of Procedure question list. Overall, however, results suggest Peter's questioning was flexible and that progressive summary refinement may have influenced that later manual. Our methodology demonstrates how computational narrative analysis illuminates textual processes shaping inquisition records, revealing how multiple voices constructed narratives of heresy.

Title: From Corpus to Narrative: Digital Reconstruction of the Early Modern Afterlife of Aratus and Cleomedes
Topics: data visualization history information retrieval literary studies network analysis philosophy statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: This proposal examines how digital methods can reconstruct the early modern transmission of Stoic cosmology through the reception of Aratus’ Phaenomena and Cleomedes’ Caelestia. Using a corpus of more than 300,000 printed sources, the study applies citation tracking, cross-lingual phrase matching, and lightweight visualisations to trace how these texts circulated between the sixteenth and eighteenth centuries. Although read primarily as scientific authorities, both works carried embedded Stoic assumptions that influenced early modern understandings of celestial order. Digital techniques reveal who cited or paraphrased these authors, when and where their works gained prominence, and how Stoic vocabulary persisted across scientific genres. The resulting visual and textual patterns form a coherent digital narrative showing how poetic-scientific texts acted as conduits for the subtle transmission of ancient cosmological ideas within early modern natural philosophy.

Title: Mining Vitae : studying hagiographical topoi on a global scale with computational methods
Topics: history literary studies religious studies statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: As part of the HagioQuant project, this research examines the Bollandists' Acta Sanctorum through large-scale computational methods to identify and analyse recurring hagiographical topoi—that is, recurring narrative themes. Since no digital version of the edition was available, we first created a complete XML corpus from an existing HTML source and carried out extensive preprocessing. This involved cleaning the files, distinguishing early Christian and medieval texts from editorial material, and applying fine-grained markup to preserve introductions, notes, dates, and references. Such enrichment enables the extraction of texts relevant to our study. Using topic modelling on this prepared corpus, we aim to trace the appearance, development, and diffusion of such topoi across the hagiographical tradition. The talk presents the workflow developed so far and highlights preliminary results from the ongoing quantitative analyses.

Authors: Katalin Suba, Tomáš Hampejs and David Zbíral
Title: Sequential variation in inquisitorial ritual narratives: a computational analysis of the consolament accounts in Register FFF
Topics: history religious studies text analysis
Abstract: This paper summarizes the results of a completed but yet unpublished study that applies computational sequence analysis to uncover variation within ritual narratives. These ritual descriptions are preserved in the FFF Register (Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, fonds Doat, vols. 22, 107r-296v; 23, 1r-346v; 24, 1r-238r), which records the inquisitorial procedures led by Friar Ferrier in Carcassonne between 1243 and 1244. Although the ritual accounts in this register appear highly formulaic at first glance – suggesting the use of a normative text during interrogations – sequence analysis reveals significant structural variation. The results indicate that, despite the presence of a standard template during questioning, deponents could draw on their own memory to add, alter, or omit elements of the ritual narrative, thus influencing the final formulation of the inquisitorial texts documenting ritual events.

Title: Beyond Numerical Dominance: Presence, Influence and Strategic Behaviour in the Dutch States-General (1626–1630)
Topics: history statistics and data science
Abstract: This paper analyzes political influence in the Dutch States-General (1626–1630) Since deliberations were not recorded, deputy presence is treated as a crucial proxy for power, moving beyond simple numerical dominance. The core methodology is the Relative Presence Profile (RPP), a computational metric that quantifies the deviation between observed and expected attendance across nearly 20,000 resolutions. RPP analysis revealed strategic, collective patterns in 'high politics': * Provinces coordinated their attendance based on the president's provincial affiliation. * The analysis challenged assumptions of dominance, showing that the Stadtholder’s confidants were often less present than expected during sessions where he was mentioned. In 'low politics,' RPP confirmed individual influence, such as the high attendance similarity observed between French ambassadors. The RPP provides a nuanced view of political influence and strategic behaviour.

Title: Tracking the Ten Thousand: Using GIS to Conceptualize Spatial Narrative in Xenophon's Anabasis
Topics: data visualization open science and public humanities spatial analysis text analysis
Abstract: Xenophon’s Anabasis is a military travelogue that recounts the journey of the so-called Ten Thousand, a Greek mercenary army hired by the Persian prince Cyrus the Younger in 401 BCE. The narrative follows the march towards the Battle of Cunaxa, where Cyrus was killed, and the retreat through hostile lands from Babylon to Pergamus. Much discourse has surrounded the inconsistencies of Xenophon’s account, and many static maps have attempted to retrace his steps. While static representations are valuable, the array of geographic information systems (GIS) technologies allows for dynamic modeling at a finer granularity. This project discusses Tracking the Ten Thousand, an ongoing project exploring the spatial narrative of the Anabasis. With full awareness of limitations this study demonstrates how GIS can be used as an interpretive framework to deepen literary analysis and reveal new insights into authorial bias.

Title: Possibilities and Tensions in Creating a Spatial Archive of the Female Survivors of Dalit Massacres in India: “Cartographies of Courage: Fictional Threads, Real Lives”
Topics: cultural studies data visualization infrastructure and tools knowledge representation literary studies open science and public humanities spatial analysis visual analysis
Abstract: This paper consolidates our practical experiences in the process of creating a spatial archive entitled, Cartographies of Courage: Fictional Threads, Real Lives (mappingmassacres.iiti.ac.in), on the female fictional and non-fictional survivors of selected Dalit massacres in India. The paper is part of a completed doctoral project at the Digital Humanities and Publishing Studies Research Group at IIT Indore. The archive includes visualizations, analyses, and findings related to space, gender, and caste dynamics in Dalit massacres. The paper examines the methods employed, challenges faced, and the impact of the archive on future research in this field. Besides the spatial archive, the project also contains educational resources and offers a workflow for the creation of Indian feminist geospatial projects.  The paper contributes to the current DH and literary scholarship in the sub-continent by offering innovative ways for sharing research on marginalized histories that can engage both academic communities and the public.

Authors: Ruhi Mahadeshwar, Tommaso Caselli, Malvina Nissim and Andreas van Cranenburgh
Title: Evaluating the Impact of Source Diversity for RAG in Historical Research
Topics: computer and data sciences GLAM history information retrieval large language models linguistics open science and public humanities text analysis
Abstract: Historical research increasingly benefits from large language models (LLMs). However, LLMs are prone to factual inaccuracy, unreliability, and biased interpretations of data. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches provide some solutions, but may inadvertently perpetuate biased perspectives embedded in historical archives. We investigate how source diversity in RAG impacts perspective variation in historical question answering. We compile a multilingual corpus of historical documents spanning multiple countries, focusing on Napoleon Bonaparte. We evaluate three Qwen3 models across ten questions using a multi-layered framework combining traditional metrics (BERTScore, ROUGE-L), frame semantics analysis, and syntactic profiling. Our results highlight that, while traditional similarity metrics suggest high semantic consistency, frame-semantic analysis exposes substantial perspective shifts. Baseline answers present ``flattened'' cross-lingual perspectives, whereas RAG introduces diversity. This diversity manifests differently across languages, demonstrating language-specific patterns. Our findings highlight limitations of traditional evaluation metrics for perspective-sensitive tasks, demonstrating that RAG constitutes active perspective transformation rather than neutral augmentation.

Title: Detecting floating stanzas in premodern Dutch songs
Topics: information retrieval large language models literary studies musicology text analysis
Abstract: This project develops a dataset of “floating stanzas” in premodern Dutch songs, stanzas that recur across seemingly unrelated pieces. Although previous studies have identified such cases, the scale and mechanisms of stanza reuse remain unexplored. Using the Dutch Song Database, the project evaluates computational methods to detect floating stanzas based on both lexical and semantic correspondences. After benchmarking these algorithms on a curated training corpus, the best-performing method will be applied to the full database. The resulting dataset will enable research on patterns of textual mobility, offering new insights into creativity, tradition, and cultural transmission in early modern song culture.

Authors: Caroline Vandyck, Mike Kestemont and Godfried Croenen
Title: The Hand of Antwerp: A Dataset of Middle Dutch Deeds (1300-1355) connected to Jan van Boendale
Topics: information retrieval literary studies text analysis
Abstract: This paper presents a dataset of 508 Middle Dutch deeds, drawn up in Antwerp between 1300 and 1355. The deeds are linked to the environment of Jan van Boendale, clerk and literary author in fourteenth-century Antwerp. Although Boendale explicitly states that he wrote Antwerp deeds, the relationship between his administrative writing and his literary oeuvre has not yet been systematically examined. Our presented dataset — comprising images, metadata, and transcriptions — enables such analysis. It compiles earlier handlists, integrates multiple sources of facsimiles, and includes both manual and HTR-generated transcriptions, the latter produced with a Kraken model trained in eScriptorium. Using this corpus, we will investigate whether Boendale’s literary works exhibit features of an administrative register. We apply computational methods for detecting intertextual parallels and compare the vocabulary of the deeds with the vocabulary in Boendale’s literary works. The dataset and HTR model will be made publicly available on Zenodo.

Title: Quill2Vec: A Tool for Vector Manipulation of Manuscripts
Topics: art history history infrastructure and tools literary studies open science and public humanities text analysis visual analysis
Abstract: Quil2Vec gives palaeographers new, no-code ways to study medieval letterforms through the lens of Scalable Vector Graphics (SVG). It is an application designed to give palaeographers the ability to create SVG versions of Medieval letterforms straight from the pages of manuscripts, as well as be a tool to split a character into its atomic components: the pen-stroke. A SVG version of a character can function as a datafied abstraction of a character and thus be used as the basis of several big data approaches to palaeography which up to now are underutilised in European Palaeography.

Authors: Nicolò Cantoni, Cornelis J. Schilt, Jeffrey C. Wolf, Demetrios Paraschos and Eszter Kovács
Title: The Stories it Tells: VERITRACE’s Interactive Metadata Explorer and New Narratives in the Reception of Ancient Wisdom
Topics: cultural studies data visualization history infrastructure and tools knowledge representation network analysis open science and public humanities philosophy religious studies statistics and data science visual analysis
Abstract: Ancient wisdom texts, from the Corpus Hermeticum to the Chaldean Oracles, profoundly shaped early modern thought, yet their circulation and editorial histories remain complex. The ERC-funded VERITRACE project employs digital-humanities methods to investigate these writings through its Close Reading Corpus (CRC), a carefully compiled collection of c. 160 Renaissance and early modern editions across six languages. By visualizing editorial networks and geographic distribution, VERITRACE uncovers overlooked translators, influential printers, and “hidden” junctures that shaped reception. For example, did Casaubon’s 1614 critique truly end Hermetic influence, or do the data suggest otherwise? The interactive Metadata Explorer allows scholars to interrogate editorial histories as empirical evidence, generating narratives that test long-standing historiographical assumptions. In this way, the CRC becomes a “storyholder,” revealing the intricate lives of these texts across intellectual and geographic contexts. This presentation demonstrates how careful digital analysis transforms metadata into rigorously grounded and compelling histories.

Authors: Gavin Lip, Julia Nobrega-Janssen, Maurice de Kleijn, Fred van Lieburg and Victor de Boer
Title: Mapping Missionaries in the Dutch colonial empire 1600-1960
Topics: history large language models religious studies social sciences statistics and data science
Abstract: Mapping Missionaries reconstructs the lives and careers of more than 7,000 religious workers active across the Dutch colonial empire (1600–1960). Building on the DigiDuRe project and multiple hand-curated missionary lists, this project integrates early modern VOC/WIC ministers and modern mission-era Zendingsarbeiders into a unified digital research infrastructure. Two complementary curation strategies were developed. First, systematic rule-based validation and manual reconciliation harmonized heterogeneous archival sources, standardized biographical attributes, identified spouses and events, and ensured provenance tracking. Second, an experimental workflow using Named Entity Recognition and Large Language Models explored context-aware extraction for semi-structured historical records, addressing variation in narrative formatting and demonstrating the potential of LLM-assisted curation for future archival work. The resulting dataset significantly expands opportunities for historical, geographical, and digital humanities research into Dutch colonialism, religion, and mobility. Future work will refine long-context LLM processing and strengthen relational linking—especially gender and family networks—to enrich the analysis of missionary communities.

Title: Functional Diversity as a Metric for Animal Diversity in Early Modern Dutch Texts: First Insights
Topics: history literary studies text analysis
Abstract: This study evaluates whether functional diversity provides a more nuanced metric for measuring animal diversity in Early Modern Dutch literature than traditional richness counts. Drawing on 1,397 DBNL texts (1500–1800) and 225,248 identified animal mentions, we compute decade-level semantic distances between animal types using GysBERT embeddings and derive functional diversity as the number of distinct functional groups. We compare this to standard richness metrics used in earlier literary biodiversity research. Although both metrics agree on periods of lowest and highest diversity, their temporal trajectories diverge significantly. Functional diversity increases steadily, while richness is more volatile. These findings indicate that metric choice strongly affects interpretations of biodiversity in texts, underscoring the need for methodological reflexivity in DH research.

Title: From Missionary Records to Data Narratives: Reconstructing the Histories of Tamil Religious Objects (1706–1741)
Topics: art history cultural studies data visualization history religious studies statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: This project examines how digital methods can reveal colonial-era trajectories of Tamil religious artefacts relocated from South India to European museums and curiosity cabinets during the Christian missionary activities of the Danish-Halle Mission in Tranquebar (1706-1741). A dataset is constructed from the ground up, utilising missionary journals, letters, and collection inventories, which enables the computational analysis of scattered and fragmented archival references. Because official records and trade documents omit illicitly transferred objects, significant archival silences are directly confronted. By digitising and structurally modelling dispersed evidence, it becomes possible to reconstruct partial object biographies and reveal previously obscured points of transfer. The study advances digitally assisted provenance research, demonstrating how digital storytelling can reveal previously overlooked narrative layers within colonial collecting practices.

Title: Beyond Archives: Archival Practice, Computational Workflows, and Digital Memorial Storytelling in a Post-War Displacement Case
Topics: data visualization GLAM history information sciences infrastructure and tools knowledge representation open science and public humanities social sciences
Abstract: This contribution presents an ongoing project that treats a post-war displacement case in Southern Europe as a laboratory for examining the interplay between archival science, computational methods, and digital memorial storytelling. The project draws on two primary corpora: institutional records produced by public authorities to govern the reception of displaced populations, and oral histories collected within community networks. Methodologically, it implements a three-stage pipeline of digitization, formalization, and publication, applied in parallel to written and oral sources. Layout segmentation and automatic text recognition for mid-twentieth-century typescripts are carried out in eScriptorium, while interviews are transcribed with speech-to-text tools and described using interoperable metadata standards. The two workflows converge in TEI-XML and are published through TEI Publisher with IIIF integration, resulting in a relational digital library. The contribution reflects on how this workflow supports the co-construction of digital and memorial narratives in a contested field of remembrance.

Title: Visual storytelling with GIS: 3D perspective Analysis of in the View of Fuzhou
Topics: spatial analysis visual analysis
Abstract: This project centres on the anonymous late-seventeenth-century drawing View of Fuzhou (Rijksmuseum, NG-1988-13) as a rare Chinese account of Sino-Dutch trade and an understudied site of Dutch presence in China. Lacking written sources on artistic practice, this project employs digital methods as storytelling tools and turns visual analysis into a narrative of Sino-Dutch cultural exchange in the early modern period. It argues that the drawing selectively adopts European, possibly Dutch, topographical conventions to visualise Dutch maritime presence. The project combines 3D elevation modelling in QGIS with a Bayesian camera-reconstruction workflow to estimate plausible viewpoints and fields of view. Preliminary analyses show how the artist compresses the Fuzhou city behind the walls while stretching the riverside foreground to highlight Nantai — the suburb where the Dutch trading post was located — and the anchored VOC ship. The project hopes to add a new digital storytelling tool to art history.

Title: The Koan Asklepieion – An Alternative Story: Approaching Healing Rituals by Serious Gaming
Topics: archaeology computer and data sciences data visualization religious studies spatial analysis visual analysis
Abstract: The Asklepieion of Kos, a key example of Hellenistic scenographic architecture, is usually interpreted through axial viewpoints and festival processions. Yet the sanctuary functioned primarily as an everyday healing centre, where pilgrims moved more freely, used side entrances, and explored votives and statues between ritual acts. To understand these non-axial, flexible movements, new visualisation methods are needed. The interdisciplinary DFG project Dynamic Spaces (2024–2026) combines architectural analysis, literary and epigraphic evidence, storytelling, and serious gaming to reconstruct such experiences. A detailed 3D model of the sanctuary, animated in Unreal Engine, simulates lighting, sound, movement, and ritual activities, enabling ground-level navigation through daily healing scenarios. This approach offers a complementary narrative to traditional festival-focused interpretations and reveals a more dynamic understanding of the sanctuary’s spatial use.

Authors: Pedro Henrique Mette Tauil, Marijn Koolen and Stefan Klut
Title: Segmentation and Linking of Seventeenth-Century States-General Correspondence
Topics: history information retrieval text analysis visual analysis
Abstract: We develop a pipeline to process incoming letters to the States-General of the Dutch Republic (1598-1700) and link them to their corresponding resolutions. The corpus comprises 119,545 scans from 201 inventory books containing copied letters. After transcription with Loghi, we focus on the 71% of text regions identified as written in Dutch. Because letters were written as continuous sequences, accurate segmentation is required. We evaluate several boundary-recognition strategies, combining greeting detection, metadata labels, normalisation with the INT-Huggingface-Tagger, and rule-based matching, achieving an F1 score of 0.88. Following segmentation, we link letters to their corresponding resolutions, which typically summarise incoming missives. Using GysBERT embeddings and cosine similarity constrained by temporal metadata, we compute pseudo-MRR scores of 0.09. Ongoing work focuses on extracting additional metadata to improve matching and to enable large-scale analysis of decision-making chains and networks of political influence in the early modern Dutch Republic.

Title: Tracing Semantic Narratives of Gratia and Fides in Neo-Latin Poetry Digital Storytelling Across Confessional Divides
Topics: computer and data sciences data visualization information retrieval linguistics religious studies statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: This study investigates how the semantics and contextual associations of gratia (‘grace’) and fides (‘faith’) evolve across time and confessional boundaries in Neo-Latin poetry from the German lands (15th–18th c.). Using a corpus of 849 texts (7M tokens) from the CAMENA Poemata collections, we apply verse-based KWIC extraction and collocational analysis (logDice) to track co-occurrence patterns across 25-year time slices and historically grounded confessional regions. By modelling diachronic variation and comparing trajectories across Catholic, Lutheran, Reformed, and mixed contexts, we reveal distinct semantic drifts tied to the Reformation and Counter-Reformation. Explicitly theological vocabulary around gratia and fides declines after 1650, while aesthetic and classical fields rise. Confessional fingerprints persist: Catholic poetry strengthens associations with amor, gloria, and Marian terminology, whereas Protestant contexts foreground lex, iustitia, and covenantal language. The project demonstrates how quantitative methods can reconstruct long-term semantic “narratives” of religious change in early modern Latin literature.

Authors: Fien Messens, Julie M. Birkholz and Christophe Verbruggen
Title: Tracing Connections: The Ego-Network of the Painter François-Joseph Navez
Topics: art history information retrieval network analysis
Abstract: This contribution to DH Benelux 2026 presents the current state of my research on the ego-network of nineteenth-century painter François-Joseph Navez. I discuss how quantitative network analyses can be meaningfully interpreted within an art-historical framework, even when the underlying data are incomplete. Building on social network analysis (SNA) methods, I demonstrate strategies for visualising uncertainty rather than concealing it, and show how partial networks can still yield robust insights. Through Navez’s ego-network, I explore how he navigated the artistic field, maintained his position, and supported others in shaping what we might now describe as their trajectories toward success.

Title: Pliny’s “Wall of Fame”: A Digital Semantic Approach to Citations and Character Networks in Books 2-6 of the Naturalis Historia
Topics: cultural studies data visualization information retrieval infrastructure and tools linguistics literary studies network analysis other statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: This oral presentation explores how semantic annotation can be leveraged to investigate the strategic use of named references in Pliny the Elder's Naturalis Historia. Specifically, it examines the relationship between mentioned individuals and the narrative features of the text. The corpus consists of books 2-6 of the Latin encyclopedia, with OCR-related errors corrected and the text linguistically and semantically annotated. By leveraging the established links with external knowledge bases, namely Wikidata and the Realencyclopädie der classischen Altertumswissenschaft, it was possible to extract information about the persons occurring in the text. Data on type, role, chronology and geography of the entities is used to explore the interconnection between named individuals and their sources in Pliny's narrative architecture.

Title: Reconstructing the Missing: Using Generative AI to Navigate Personal Fragmented Holocaust Records
Topics: computer and data sciences history information retrieval knowledge representation large language models linguistics text analysis
Abstract: My grandmother once told me she had never read any of her father’s articles. Her father, my great-grandfather, was a Yiddish journalist in Berlin and later in the Netherlands, yet somewhere between war, displacement and loss, his voice vanished from our family. I wanted to know whether his writing had truly disappeared, so I turned to the tools I work with every day, AI and large language models, to see what could still be recovered. This talk presents a personal experiment: using LLMs to navigate digitised Yiddish, cope with unreliable OCR, interpret inconsistent spellings and metadata, and trace scattered evidence across collections. I will discuss what these models were genuinely helpful for, where they failed, and how it felt to see his words appear on my screen for the first time. The session concludes with reusable patterns for applying AI to complex, multilingual and emotionally charged historical material.

Authors: Jona Schlegel and Thunnis van Oort
Title: Linking Surinamese Heritage Data: Building a Community-Focused Platform
Topics: data visualization history open science and public humanities
Abstract: This poster presents the initial phase of a project that connects nineteenth-century historical data from Suriname with a citizen science approach. The work focuses on gathering and structuring heterogeneous datasets from archival records held across various heritage institutions, including population registers, almanacs, images, and annotated historical maps. A central goal is to prepare these datasets so they support both scholarly research and exploration by (Dutch-)Surinamese communities. The project develops a scalable data model based on W3C standards and Linked Open Data principles. In a second phase, a user-friendly, multilingual interface will be created, building on an existing map-annotation environment and emphasising accessibility, clarity, and transparent provenance linked to the curating institution. The poster presents the current development stage, including early visualisation prototypes, and invites conference participants to engage with the publicly accessible prototype.

Authors: Ortal-Paz Saar, Korshi Dosoo, Raquel Martín Hernández and Panagiota Sarischouli
Title: Untold Stories: The Text that Wasn’t Text
Topics: cultural studies history linguistics religious studies text analysis visual analysis
Abstract: Magical texts from the ancient and medieval Mediterranean region attract increasing scholarly attention, giving rise to new interpretative issues as well as questions. In this paper (part of a larger joint project, titled “Structuring Magic”) we present some of the key issues, and the ways in which interdisciplinary DH projects can answer these questions. The paper focuses on “texts that aren’t there”, that is, an important phenomenon found in magical texts which is not textual in itself: the magical signs (‘charakteres’). While not being a textual element in the traditional sense, these signs are nevertheless part of the communication between ritual practitioners and the entities they were addressing. The paper present a new digital method for unveiling their untold stories.

Authors: Karina van Dalen-Oskam, Bram Oostveen, Joris J. van Zundert, Henny Brandhorst, Menno Metselaar and Marco Streefkerk
Title: Stylistic Influences in Anne Frank’s Writings
Topics: literary studies text analysis
Abstract: We examine which of the books Anne Frank read influenced her own writing style in a measurable way, focusing on the manuscript that Anne Frank intended to become the novel The Back House (B). In our first use case, we compare the texts written by Anne Frank with a small corpus including the four Joop ter Heul volumes by Cissy van Marxveldt that are often mentioned as most influential on her work. The results show that the novels by Van Marxveldt are not the closest ones to her own works, but those by De Clercq Zubli. Rolling stylometry suggests that De Clercq Zubli’s influence mainly resides in function words, whereas Van Marxveldt’s influence is strongest in content words. In our second case study we will replicate the research on a larger corpus, using different methods, to explore which concrete linguistic forms this influence took.

Title: Using Large Language Models to understand ‘the masses’ in nineteenth-century Dutch newspapers
Topics: history large language models text analysis
Abstract: This study investigates the emergence and representation of “the masses” in nineteenth-century Dutch newspapers, using computational methods to distinguish between physical crowds and abstract notions of mass society. We employ a multi-step classification pipeline, fine-tuning BERT-based models on annotated samples from the Delpher newspaper corpus. The workflow includes human-in-the-loop annotation, binary and multi-class classification, and filtering for relevant contexts. Results show high accuracy (>90%) for key tasks and reveal that abstract references to “the masses” consistently outnumber mentions of physical gatherings, though the latter peaks around 1860. These findings challenge assumptions about the timing and nature of mass discourse. Future work will explore sentiment analysis and extend the approach to other corpora, enabling large-scale historical research into political language and conceptual change.

Authors: Jeroen Salman, Julian Gonggrijp and Tijmen Baarda
Title: A virtual research environment for collaborative querying, collecting and annotating works of European popular print
Topics: GLAM infrastructure and tools knowledge representation literary studies
Abstract: We present a system to collaboratively query, collect and annotate records from libraries and cultural heritage institutions from around Europe. This system is set up as a virtual research environment (VRE) and has been created as part of an international research project that asks the question to what extent popular print culture in the period 1450–1900 was a European phenomenon with a transnational infrastructure. In our VRE, users can search and work with multiple catalogues from libraries and cultural heritage institutions, as well as a number of databases created by researchers. Users can add the records they find to private or public collections, and add annotations to the records as a whole or to their individual fields, and classify the records according to a glossary of genres. Data is collected on the fly from external databases and APIs and is internally stored as linked data in RDF format.

Authors: Sviatoslav Drach, Benedikte Löbbert and Claes Neuefeind
Title: Presenting research in an engaging way: digital exhibitions as a sustainable publication format
Topics: archaeology history infrastructure and tools knowledge representation
Abstract: Digital exhibitions have great potential as complementary research-related services and can serve as a sustainable publication format. To ensure that the digital exhibition meets the standards of a sustainable publication, several aspects had to be considered. In this article, we introduce a digital exhibition of Byzantine lead seals, gathered from different collections around the world, and discuss it with the DH Benelux community in terms of best practices in DH research (such as compliance with the FAIR principles) and its potential as a sustainable publication format.

Authors: Manuela Ritondale, Ruhi Mahadeshwar and Malvina Nissim
Title: Narratives of maritime risk: a Natural Language Processing approach to reassess ancient seafaring strategies in the Mediterranean Sea
Topics: archaeology history linguistics spatial analysis statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: This study employs Natural Language Processing (NLP) to reassess ancient Mediterranean seafaring strategies by examining how risk was described and embedded in textual narratives. While seafaring models often assume that ancient mariners favoured the safest routes, such assumptions overlook variable perceptions of danger. We apply NLP techniques to Greek and Latin sources that reference navigational practices and seaborne movements in three crucial maritime regions: the Gulf of Lion, the Tyrrhenian coast and Cape Malea. Combining close reading with topic modelling and machine-learning classification, we identify linguistic patterns associated with seafaring risk and explore their non-causal correspondence with shipwreck densities. Explainable AI methods further highlight which textual features shape classifier predictions, revealing intersections between narrative constructions of risk, archaeological evidence and environmental conditions. By integrating computational analysis with philological and archaeological expertise, this study challenges prevailing assumptions in seafaring models and offers a refined framework for understanding ancient navigational behaviour.

Title: Digital Echoes: Narrating Galen’s Theory of Voice in a Digital Interface
Topics: cultural studies data visualization history information retrieval knowledge representation large language models linguistics other philosophy text analysis
Abstract: This project demonstrates how complex philological research can be transformed into an interactive digital narrative through a case study of Galen’s theory of phonation. Galen’s account, dispersed across multiple treatises, is both conceptually dense and dynamically structured, emphasising processes such as ekphusēsis, glottal narrowing, and the striking of the laryngeal cartilages. To communicate this sequential model, I developed a digital interface that presents voice production as a guided narrative, integrating philological research work and manually authored scholarly visualisations into a unified interpretive environment. These multimodal elements appear precisely where they illuminate textual explanations, revealing the narrative logic underlying Galen’s physiology. The interface is generated through a hybrid workflow in which AI interprets structured analytical text and proposes visual sequences, while human oversight ensures fidelity to the ancient sources. The project argues that interface design can function as a mode of knowledge production, making complex historical science newly accessible and coherent.

Title: Quantitative Stemmatology and Open Recensio: A Test Case on the Bible historiale’s Book of Exodus
Topics: computer and data sciences infrastructure and tools literary studies open science and public humanities text analysis
Abstract: This paper examines the contribution of quantitative stemmatology to a medieval tradition marked by a Pasqualian open recensio. Eight witnesses of the Bible historiale transmitting Exodus were encoded in TEI with an apparatus recording lexical, syntactic, morphological and macro-structural variation. From this encoding, genealogical variant locationswere extracted and modelled in R. The experiment evaluates how computational methods interpret a stratified and contaminated tradition and compares the results with Bénédicte Michel’s qualitative hypotheses for the Genèse. Preliminary analyses reveal stable clusters crossing redactional phases and show where modelling supports philological iudicium and where the tradition resists mechanical reconstruction.

Title: Feeling the Ancient Pulse: An AI-Assisted Interactive Platform for Experiencing Galenic Pulse Theory
Topics: data visualization history infrastructure and tools large language models literary studies philosophy visual analysis
Abstract: This lightning talk presents an AI-assisted interactive platform that makes ancient pulse diagnosis perceptible to contemporary users by transforming Galenic pulse theory into an experiential format. Serving as a digital bridge between textual descriptions and the clinical phenomenon, the interface visualizes perceptual dimensions that ancient authors assumed but could not fully express in words. Interactive simulations translate descriptors such as the “gazelle-like” or “ant-like” pulse into dynamic visualizations. Three narrative layers structure the platform: physiological animations reconstruct Galen’s pneumatic model; palpation guides demonstrate diagnostic techniques; and taxonomic maps clarify relationships across Galen’s pulse treatises. Arguing that visualization is interpretive scholarship rather than neutral display, the platform highlights design choices as analytical interventions. Developed through human–AI collaboration, the project uses natural-language interaction to create animated components and interface logic. The talk demonstrates how this approach broadens digital storytelling methods and expands possibilities in digital humanities.

Title: "Seeing" Direct Speech Differently in Victorian Novels: A Text-Visualization Case Study of David Copperfield
Topics: data visualization information retrieval large language models literary studies statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: Recent Digital Humanities scholarship has drawn attention to the significant amount of direct speech – a simple formal technique that has long been ignored by scholars and overshadowed by free indirect discourse – in Victorian novels. At the same time, the development of large language models has made it increasingly efficient to retrieve information from a literary corpus. Building on the growing interest in direct speech, my project uses computational methods to examine how the representation of fictional voices intersects with class. By deploying BookNLP to automatically extract quotes with coreference and mapping out the speech features of each character in the sample corpus, David Copperfield, I argue that class relationship is inscribed in the Victorian characters’ oral landscape. In my proposed presentation, I will focus on justifying the DH methods I used, highlighting their limitations, and discussing how text-visualization techniques can both generate research insights and support literary interpretation.

Title: Modelling Crisis at Scale: Text Mining the Crisification of Events in Western Societies, 1800-2025
Topics: history large language models statistics and data science text analysis
Abstract: This paper develops a framework for analysing how crises take shape in Western societies from 1800 to 2025. It discusses the way in which humanities theory can be leveraged to study the long-term history of crisis with computational methods. In doing so, we depart from the notion of crises as "crisified" events. We assert that societies construct events as crises through narratives. We identify four mechanisms that structure this process: symbolization, dramatization, legitimization, and problematization. Methodologically, we first detect "focus events" by modelling interpretative ambiguity through measures of diachronic novelty and synchronic semantic coherence. We then construct event-centered subcorpora using hybrid semantic retrieval techniques. From these, we extract features such as contentious labels, actor networks, and evolving policy frames to trace the four dynamics of crisification over time. The paper presents initial results from event detection, subcorpus construction, and feature extraction, demonstrating how crises can be modelled at scale.

Authors: Marta Kipke, Rie Schmidt Eriksen, Kristoffer Nielbo and Katrine Baunvig
Title: The Narrative of National Romanticism in 19th Century Painting: The Danish Golden Age and Beyond
Topics: art history data visualization visual analysis
Abstract: The Danish Golden Age (ca. 1800-1850) is renowned for its landscape painting, which depicts an idealized version of the region and is closely linked to historical events and national identity building during a turbulent time in northern Europe. At the same time, national romanticism also flourished in other neighbouring countries, notably Germany, Norway, Sweden and Finland. Despite the strong connection of national romanticism to its region and current political events, artists were mobile and traveled to different hubs – such as Copenhagen, Dresden and Düsseldorf – to hone their craft. In the light of these entangled relationships, we investiagte the nature of national romanticism between style, motif and story telling. We implement machine learning and feature space exploration methods to investigate art historical storytelling in relation to visualization and statistical processing. By doing so, we aim to reconsider the very notion of national romanticism as an evolving narrative.

Title: Reconstructing Ancient Networks: Integrating Corpus Analysis with Wikidata
Topics: history literary studies network analysis philosophy
Abstract: This paper compares two networks of individuals drawn from Greek and Latin literature and enriched with linked open data. The first network is constructed from textual co-occurrences, capturing how ancient authors positioned individuals in relation to one another. The second derives from Wikidata, where structured interlinks such as familial relations and student-teacher connections provide explicit relational metadata.
