By juxtaposing these networks, I investigate which individuals appear in Wikidata but not in the literary corpus and vice-versa, and how familial and pedagogical ties compare to the topology of the literature based network. Last, I explore whether edge qualifications from Wikidata can be projected back onto co-occurrence edges in the corpus, thereby enriching textual associations with semantic depth.

Authors: Fei Fei, Kenzo Milleville, Vincent Ducatteeuw, Rein Debrulle, Helena Van Hiel, Iason Jongepier, Léa Hermenault, Christophe Verbruggen, Steven Verstockt and Dieter De Witte
Title: Reading the Landscape: Data Extraction Using Computer Vision for the Artemis Project
Topics: computer and data sciences data visualization GLAM history information retrieval information sciences infrastructure and tools spatial analysis statistics and data science visual analysis
Abstract: This paper presents initial results from the Artemis project, which builds open digital infrastructure for historical maps of the Scheldt River Valley. The project focuses on the Primitive and Reduced Cadastre series, which record parcel boundaries and textual annotations from the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. We combine georeferencing, text recognition and parcel segmentation to convert archival maps into usable research data. For contour extraction we fine tuned a UNet++ model on nine annotated sheets and compared it with a publicly available Mask2Former model. The fine tuned model achieved a Dice score of 0.633 and an IoU of 0.463 on the validation sheet. A morphological closing step improved polygon extraction while still leaving incomplete parcels. For text recognition we fine tuned the MapReader model on our annotations and reduced the character error rate from 27.8 to 2.13 percent. These results outline clear next steps for improving automated processing of historical cartography.

Title: Automatically Transcribing the Archives of Henri Pirenne
Topics: history open science and public humanities
Abstract: This lightning talk presents the development of a Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) model to automatically transcribe the handwriting of the Belgian historian Henri Pirenne. Initially conceived to transcribe and compare the various versions of Mahomet et Charlemagne for genetic analysis, the HTR model enables large-scale processing of Pirenne’s broader archives at the ULB, including lecture notes, course materials, correspondence, and research documents. This new access opens avenues for computational exploration of his intellectual work. The talk will outline the main stages of building the HTR model with the open-source tool Kraken, detailing the hyperparameters and best practices used to generate high-quality ground truth efficiently. It will also present a transcription guide, an open dataset, and the range of computational analyses enabled by this corpus.

Title: Read and See: Dual Approaches to Content Representation in the Digital Edition Buber-Korrespondenzen Digital
Topics: cultural studies data visualization knowledge representation network analysis text analysis visual analysis
Abstract: The paper will present ongoing research from the long-term Martin Buber-Korrespondenzen Digital project, which will edit over 41,000 letters by and to Martin Buber. To manage the huge volume of letters, a modular edition approach with three tiers of editorial depth is applied. The oral presentation will focus on the intermediate tier, where a dual "Read and See" methodology is implemented: letters receive both human-readable summaries in plain language and machine-readable structured representations using entity-relation triples. This evidence-based approach captures only relationships explicitly documented in each letter, aligning with Georg Vogeler's concept of the assertive edition. The project uses TEI <relation> elements to model relationships and employs Neo4j for macro-level network analysis and Mermaid.js for micro-level visualization of individual letters. This dual approach bridges traditional humanistic scholarship with digital humanities methods, exemplifying how narrative summaries and computational modelling can serve complementary purposes in digital scholarly editions.

Authors: Friederike Voit, Gleb Schmidt and Sven Meeder
Title: "Blessed are the Poor": Modelling Christian Values in Early Medieval Canon Law (400-1100)
Topics: history religious studies text analysis
Abstract: Deploying methods inspired by topic modelling, this paper presents early results of a study exploring the dynamics of Christianisation and social justice in early medieval canon law. It investigates the hypothesis that, between the fourth century, when Christianity was established as the official religion of the Roman Empire, and the end of the Carolingian era in the tenth century, the values expressed in legal discourse became progressively Christianised, as evident in an increasing focus on care for poor and vulnerable groups. We aim to investigate the prevalence of the semantic field related to social justice as well as its shifting components across early medieval canon law.

Title: (Re-)telling 12th century papal history
Topics: history knowledge representation text analysis
Abstract: The proposed presentation examines the development of a data model designed to enable a new, quantitative approach to analysing the information encoded in medieval charters. While the encoding of persons and places is relatively straight forward, the encoding and modelling of events and legal acts is a more complex task. With our presentation we want to discuss our proof of concept for encoding acts mentioned in papal charters and their relation to general historic events of that time.

Title: From Notes to Nodes: Interactive Modeling and Visualization of Bertini’s Dictionary of Musicians (1814)
Topics: computer and data sciences data visualization history knowledge representation musicology network analysis text analysis
Abstract: The lightning talk presents a methodological framework developed to transform Girolamo Bertini’s Dizionario storico-critico degli scrittori di musica e de’ più celebri artisti (1814) into an interactive network-based visualization system for exploratory and distant reading. The four-volume biographical dictionary is partly based on the French Dictionnaire historique des musiciens (1810-11), but Bertini expands the model by adding a significant number of entries devoted to Italian composers and musicians. While Bertini’s work represents an important resource for musical historiography, the linear structure of the text makes it difficult to perceive broader relational patterns among the entries. Following the perspective of distant reading introduced by Moretti (2000; 2013), the aim of this study is to reframe sequences of isolated narrative units as part of larger relational structures.

Title: Stories telling data telling histories: digital methods for exploring intercultural stereotypes in Old French fiction
Topics: cultural studies history information retrieval linguistics literary studies network analysis text analysis
Abstract: This lightning talk will present an early-stage research that tackles Old French epics and novels as sources for understanding intercultural stereotypes in the European Middle Ages, and specifically the Western attitude towards Byzantium in the period of the Crusades (11th-13th century). We propose a digital workflow with an ultimate goal of a cross-genre sentiment analysis, addressing challenges such as limited digitized corpora, scarce NLP tools for Old French, and identity disambiguation. Preliminary solutions include using narrative analysis through distant reading and the modern French intermediary, and a relational database to model nested levels of fictionality typical of these medieval stories.

Title: Flora and the imperial machine: storytelling colonial ecology from historical botanical exchange data
Topics: data visualization history network analysis text analysis visual analysis
Abstract: This poster presents an ongoing effort to develop digital humanities methods for modelling transcribed plant exchange ledgers into larger scale narratives about the ecological change worked by colonial empires on a global scale. The development of histories of imperial botany has developed many important narratives of trade, calculation, and extraction, but within these the key methodological difficulty is working with a multitude of living plants towards environmental storytelling. Following individual plants through archival records reveals the importance of each of these stories within a network of plants picked, propagated, and ecologically reconfigured. Multivarious plant exchange between botanists, colonists, gardeners, and imperial citizens reveal the living worlds that spread around and outside larger institutions. Together, this shows how colonial plant exchange transferred regional ecology into a global botanical system that was itself a new kind of ecology at a global scale.

Authors: Sara Budts, Rik Vosters and Yoshi Malaise
Title: Using transformer models to analyze orthographic variation in Late Modern Dutch witness depositions
Topics: history linguistics text analysis
Abstract: his contribution presents a cookbook recipe to analyze orthographic variation and standardization synchronically and diachronically using transformers. Most diachronic studies to date have measured orthographic standardization by comparing historical spellings to modern equivalents after normalization and lemmatization (e.g. Tyrkkö 2020). Such approaches depict the process as a straightforward ‘dialect-to-standard’ trajectory that Watts (2011) criticized as a “funnel view” on standardization. After unpacking this teleological bias, we propose a novel heuristic that departs from historical variation instead. We apply our approach to the Witnesses corpus, a collection of 18th- and 19th-century Flemish witness depositions. Drawing on GysBERT (Manjavacas & Fonteyn 2022), we generate word pairs of historical spelling variants, manually annotate them and subsequently finetune the model iteratively to arrive at variant clusters of likely historical spelling variants. We then quantify orthographic variation across texts, testing diachronic patterns and differences by text type, scribe, and other sociolinguistic variables.
